Q1: Honestly, I didn't need to organize my study routine too rigidly because things just kind of flowed naturally for me. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around the house or during my commute. I found that about 12 hours a week was plenty to keep up with everything. I'd batch my assignment work and knock it out efficiently rather than spreading it thin across the week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I didn't stick to a strict schedule because I didn't really need to—I could pick things up quickly when I focused. When deadlines approached, I'd ramp up, but otherwise I kept things pretty relaxed. It worked well for me since I never felt overwhelmed.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment details or submit something. Sometimes I'd pop in to download lecture recordings so I could listen to them later on my phone. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS for fun or anything—I went in with a purpose and got out pretty quickly.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. I'd check it maybe once or twice a week, grab what I needed, and then do my actual studying elsewhere. It was more of a tool I used strategically rather than something integrated into my routine.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I loved being able to listen to them multiple times while walking or relaxing. The assignment submission portal was obviously necessary, and I appreciated when instructions were clear and straightforward. Those were really the main things I relied on.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I only posted twice the whole semester. It's not that I thought they were useless, but I just didn't feel the need to engage there to understand the material. I also didn't watch videos directly on the platform much; I preferred downloading things and accessing them offline.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly contributed to my understanding or my grade. I'm pretty efficient with my time, so I'd quickly assess whether something would actually help me or if it was just busywork. If I could see a clear connection to the assessments or my learning goals, I was all in.
Q8: Optional activities felt exactly that—optional. I'd glance at them and decide whether they'd add value, and usually I concluded I was fine without them. Required activities got my full attention because they directly impacted my grade. I didn't feel guilty skipping optional stuff since my results showed I was doing well without them.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen on my phone, sometimes multiple times. I also used textbooks and external resources I found through quick searches. My notes were all handwritten or in a separate app, so none of that showed up in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing concepts by re-listening to lectures while taking walks or doing chores. I'd also discuss course content with friends outside of class, which was really valuable for solidifying my understanding. None of that active learning would show up as LMS activity at all.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture recording once to download it, then listen to it three or four more times offline over the following days. The LMS would only show that single access, but in reality, I was deeply engaging with that material repeatedly. It was just more convenient to have everything on my phone.
Q12: There were brief moments when I felt a bit disconnected, usually when the material got dry or when I had a lot going on with extracurriculars. But honestly, it never lasted long. I'd do a quick mindfulness exercise to reset my focus, and then I'd bounce back pretty easily.
Q13: Sometimes when I had competitions or events for my extracurriculars, I'd delay logging into the LMS for a few days. But it never really affected my performance because I'd catch up quickly once things settled down. I'm fortunate that I have good resources and support, so competing responsibilities were manageable.
Q14: Occasionally I'd scroll through materials on the LMS without really absorbing anything—like when I was tired or distracted. Those moments were rare though. Usually if I was accessing something, I was genuinely trying to learn from it, even if my engagement looked minimal from the outside.
Q15: Sometimes I'd postpone discussion posts because they felt less important than other assignments. I knew they mattered for participation, but I'd prioritize things that carried more weight grade-wise. I don't think it hurt me much in the end since my assignment and exam scores were strong.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main trigger for LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as submission dates approached, download what I needed, and complete everything with time to spare. Between deadlines, my LMS presence dropped significantly because I was studying through other means.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to maintain my approach rather than change it. When I saw high scores coming back, I felt validated that my study methods were working. I didn't need to adjust much because the results spoke for themselves. Positive feedback kept me feeling happy and confident.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I appreciated. Having materials available to download meant I could learn on my own terms. The clear assignment guidelines helped me work efficiently. I didn't need a lot of hand-holding, so the structure suited my independent learning style well.
Q19: The availability of audio recordings made engagement much easier for someone like me who learns best by listening. What made it slightly harder was that the discussion forums felt disconnected from my learning process—they seemed designed for a different type of learner. Overall though, the design worked in my favor.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to find it, so they keep clicking around without producing work. Or maybe they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing tasks. It could also be that they're exploring out of curiosity but not committing to the assignments.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I earned a Distinction because my real learning happened offline. I listened to recordings repeatedly, studied from downloaded materials, and worked through problems independently. The LMS just doesn't capture that kind of deep, personal engagement.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show submission patterns and deadline compliance, which they captured well for me. But they completely miss offline study, repeated engagement with downloaded content, and the quality of understanding someone develops. They show presence, not learning depth.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and putting in thoughtful effort to master it. For me, it's about active listening, connecting concepts, and performing well on assessments. It's not about how many times I click on things—it's about whether the knowledge actually sticks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how many times I replayed lecture audio while walking my dog, or the conversations I had with classmates about course concepts. It wouldn't capture my genuine happiness with the subject matter or how the mindfulness practices helped me stay centered. My real engagement lived outside that platform.
